title: DMSANet: Dual Multi Scale Attention Network

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - DMSANET: DUAL MULTI SCALE ATTENTION NET WORK

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Attention mechanism of late has been quite popular in the computer vision community. A lot of work has been done to improve the performance of the network, although almost always it results in increased computational complexity. In this paper, we propose a new attention module that not only achieves the best performance but also has lesser parameters compared to most existing models. Our attention module can easily be integrated with other convolutional neural networks because of its lightweight nature. The proposed network named Dual Multi Scale Attention Network (DMSANet) is comprised of two parts: the first part is used to extract features at various scales and aggregate them, the second part uses spatial and channel attention modules in parallel to adaptively integrate local features with their global dependencies. We benchmark our network performance for Image Classification on ImageNet dataset, Object Detection and Instance Segmentation both on MS COCO dataset.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The local receptive field of the human eye has led to the construction of convolutional neural networks which has powered much of the recent advances in computer vision. Multi scale architecture used in the famous InceptionNet (Szegedy et al., 2016) aggregates multi-scale information from different size convolutional kernels. Attention Networks has attracted a lot of attention recently as it allows the network to focus on only then essential aspects while ignoring the ones which are not useful (Li et al., 2019), (Cao et al., 2019) and (Li et al., 2019).

A lot of problems have been successfully tackled using attention mechanism in computer vision like image classification, image segmentation, object detection and image generation. Most of the attention mechanisms can be broadly classified into two types channel attention and spatial attention, both of which strengthens the original features by aggregating the same feature from all the positions with different aggregation strategies, transformations, and strengthening functions (Zhang et al., 2021).

Some of the work combined both these mechanism together and achieved better results (Cao et al., 2019) and (Woo et al., 2018). The computational burden was reduced by (Wang et al., 2020) using efficient channel attention and 1 _×_ 1 convolution. The most popular attention mechanism is the Squeeze-and Excitation module (Hu et al., 2018b), which can significantly improve the performance with a considerably low cost. The “channel shuffle” operator is used (Zhang and Yang, 2021) to enable information communication between the two branches. It uses a grouping strategy, which divides the input feature map into groups along the channel dimension.

## 2 RELATED WORK

There are two main problems which hinders the progress in this field: 1) Both spatial and channel attention as well as network using combination of two uses only local information while ignoring long range channel dependency, 2) The previous architectures fail to capture spatial information at different scales to be more robust and handle more complex problems. These two challenges were tackled by (Duta et al., 2020) and (Li et al., 2019) respectivly. The problem with these architectures is that the number of parameters increased considerably.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Pyramid Split Attention (PSA) (Zhang et al., 2021) has the ability to process the input tensor at multiple scales. A multi-scale pyramid convolution structure is used to integrate information at different scales on each channel-wise feature map. The channel-wise attention weight of the multi-scale feature maps are extracted hence long range channel dependency is done.

Non-Local block (Wang et al., 2018) is proposed to build a dense spatial feature map and capture the long-range dependency using non-local operations. (Li et al., 2019) used a dynamic selection attention mechanism that allows each neuron to adaptively adjust its receptive field size based on multiple scales of input feature map. (Fu et al., 2019) proposed a network to integrate local features with their global dependencies by summing these two attention modules from different branches.

Multi scale architectures have been used sucessfully for a lot of vision problems ( **?** ), (Hu et al., 2018b) and (Sagar and Soundrapandiyan, 2020). (Fu et al., 2019) adaptively integrated local features with their global dependencies by summing the two attention modules from different branches. (Hu et al., 2018a) used spatial extension using a depth-wise convolution to aggregate individual features. Our network borrows ideas from (Gao et al., 2018) which used a network to capture local cross-channel interactions.
The performance (in terms of accuracy) vs computational complexity (in terms of number of parameters) of the state of art attention modules is shown in Figure 1:

Figure 1: Comparing the accuracy of different attention methods with ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 as backbone in terms of accuracy and network parameters. The circles reflects the network parameters and FLOPs of different models. Our proposed network achieves higher accuracy while having less model complexity.

Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:

• A new attention module is proposed which aggregates feature information at various scales. Our network is scalable and can be easily plugged into various computer vision problems.

- Our network captures more contextual information using both spatial and channel attention at

- various scales.
• Our experiments demonstrate that our network outperforms previous state of the art with lesser computational cost.

## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 FEATURE GROUPING

Shuffle Attention module divides the input feature map into groups and uses Shuffle Unit to integrate the channel attention and spatial attention into one block for each group. The sub-features are aggregated and a “channel shuffle” operator is used for communicating the information between different sub-features.

For a given feature map _X ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ , where _C, H, W_ indicate the channel number, spatial height, and width, respectively, shuffle attention module divides _X_ into _G_ groups along the channel dimension, i.e., _X_ = [ _X_ 1 _, XG_ ] _, Xk ∈ R[C/G][×][H][×][W]_ . An attention module is used to weight the importance of each feature. The input of _Xk_ is split into two networks along the channel dimension _Xk_ 1 _, Xk_ 2 _∈ R[C/]_[2] _[G][×][H][×][W]_ . The first branch is used to produce a channel attention map by using the relationship of channels, while the second branch is used to generate a spatial attention map by using the spatial relationship of different features.

## 3.2 CHANNEL ATTENTION MODULE

The channel attention module is used to selectively weight the importance of each channel and thus produces best output features. This helps in reducing the number of parameters of the network. Let _X ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ denotes the input feature map, where the quantity _H, W, C_ represent its height, width and number of input channels respectively. A SE block consists of two parts: squeeze and excitation, which are respectively designed for encoding the global information and adaptively recalibrating the channel-wise relationship. We calculate the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ from the original features _A ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We reshape A to _R[C][×][N]_ , and then perform a matrix multiplication between A and the transpose of A. ## 3.4 AGGREGATION

In the final part of the network, all the sub-features are aggregated. We use a ”channel shuffle” operator to enable cross-group information flow along the channel dimension. The final output of our module is the same size as that of input, making our attention module quite easy to integrate with other networks.

## 3.5 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

We propose DMSA module with the goal to build more efficient and scalable architecture. The first part of our network borrows ideas from (Li et al., 2019) and (Zhang and Yang, 2021). An input feature map _X_ is splitted into _N_ parts along with the channel dimension. For each splitted parts, it has _C_ 0= _CS_ number of common channels, and the _i[th]_ feature map is _Xi ∈ R[C]_[0] _[×][H][×][W]_ . The individual features are fused before being passed to two different branches.

These two branches are comprised of position attention module and channel attention module as proposed in (Fu et al., 2019) for semantic segmentation. The second part of our network does the following 1) Builds a spatial attention matrix which models the spatial relationship between any

## 3.6 IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS

We use Residual Networks (He et al., 2016) as the backbone which is widely used in literature for image classification on Imagenet dataset (Deng et al., 2009). Data augmentation is used for increasing the size of the dataset and the input tensor is cropped to size 224 _×_ 224. Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.

We use Residual Network along with FPN as the backbone network (Lin et al., 2017a) for object detection. The detectors we benchmark against are Faster RCNN (Ren et al., 2015), Mask RCNN(He et al., 2017) and RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017b) on MS-COCO dataset (Lin et al., 2014). Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with a weight decay of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum is 0.9, and the batch size is 16 per GPU for 10 epochs. The learning rate is set as 0.01 and is decreased by the factor of 10 every 10th epoch.

For instance segmentation we use Mask RCNN (He et al., 2017) with FPN (Lin et al., 2017a) as backbone. Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with a weight decay of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum is 0.9, and the batch size is 4 per GPU for 10 epochs. The learning rate is set as 0.01 and is decreased by the factor of 10 every 10th epoch.

## 4.4 ABLATION STUDY

The ablation studies of our architecture is shown in Table 8. The results are best obtained using instance normalization. Both removing _Fc_ () and using 1 _×_ 1 Conv results in reduced performance as compared to the original network. The earlier is because _Fc_ () is used to enhance the performance of individual features while latter is because number of channels in each sub-feature is too few, so it is not important to exchange information among different channels.

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we propose a novel Attention module named Dual Multi Scale Attention Network (DMSANet). Our network is comprised of two parts 1) first for aggregating feature information at various scales 2) second made up of position and channel attention modules in parallel for capturing global contextual information. After evaluating our network both qualitatively and quantitatively, we show that our network outperforms previous state of the art across image classification, object

detection and instance segmentation problems. The ablation experiments show that our attention module captures long-range contextual information effectively at various scales thus making it generalizable to other tasks. The best part of DMSANet attention module is that it is very lightweight and hence could be easily plugged into various custom networks as and when required.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes DMSANet, a dual multi-scale attention network that claims strong performance improvements over existing attention mechanisms in image classification, object detection, and instance segmentation, with lower computational cost. The idea of combining multi-scale processing with parallel spatial-channel attention is well-motivated by prior work and aligns with current trends in efficient attention design. The reported results, particularly the large accuracy gains on ImageNet and COCO, are impressive if valid.

Overview issues:

- Missing ablation study on core architectural components: The ablation study (Table 8) only evaluates normalization variants and the replacement of the $F_c()$ operation, but does not isolate the contribution of the dual attention mechanisms (spatial vs. channel) or the multi-scale feature grouping. This is a major flaw because the paper's central claim is the effectiveness of dual multi-scale attention. Without ablations that remove or disable the spatial attention branch, the channel attention branch, or the multi-scale aggregation, it is impossible to determine which components are responsible for the performance gains. The current ablation does not support the paper's main claims and fails to meet standard evaluation practices in the field.

- Unclear and potentially incorrect formulation of attention modules: The paper presents conflicting or ambiguous definitions of the channel and spatial attention modules. Equation 3 defines a $C \times C$ channel attention map using dot products of reshaped features, resembling non-local operations, yet this is not reconciled with the SE-like formulation in Equation 2. Similarly, the spatial attention in Equation 5 computes an $N \times N$ matrix (where $N = H \times W$), which is computationally infeasible for typical feature maps (e.g., $56 \times 56$ implies $3136^2$ elements). No complexity analysis or approximation is provided. These formulations appear inconsistent with efficient attention design and raise doubts about the actual implementation behind the reported results.

- Inadequate comparison with state-of-the-art attention mechanisms: The paper compares DMSANet with older or less competitive baselines (e.g., SENet, CBAM, ECANet) but omits recent, more relevant architectures such as CoordAttention, SimAM, or even the closely related SA-Net and EPSANet variants that are designed for efficiency. While EPSANet is included, the comparison lacks depth—no discussion of architectural differences or why DMSANet outperforms it. Given the claims of superior efficiency and accuracy, the absence of comparisons with the most competitive lightweight attention modules undermines the significance of the contribution.

- Unsubstantiated claims about computational efficiency: The paper claims DMSANet has 'lesser parameters' and 'lower computational cost' than existing models, citing FLOPs and parameter counts in Tables 2 and 3. However, the FLOPs for attention modules are typically negligible compared to convolutions, and the reported FLOPs differences (e.g., 3.44G vs 4.12G for ResNet-50) likely reflect changes in the backbone or multi-scale processing, not the attention module alone. Without isolating the cost of the DMSA block, the efficiency claims are misleading. Moreover, inference speed or latency measurements on hardware are absent, which are critical for assessing real-world efficiency.

- Lack of clarity in network architecture and feature grouping: The feature grouping strategy (Section 3.1) is vaguely described—how many groups are used? How are features split and recombined? The 'channel shuffle' operator is mentioned but not clearly integrated into the overall flow. Figure 2 is too schematic to clarify these details. This lack of specificity makes the method difficult to reproduce and raises concerns about the feasibility of cross-group communication. The grouping strategy is central to the design, yet its rationale and impact on performance or efficiency are not analyzed.

- Results section lacks methodological transparency: The paper reports strong performance gains (e.g., 80.02% Top-1 accuracy with ResNet-50) that exceed typical baselines by a wide margin, yet the 'Results' section is empty of any discussion or analysis. Tables 4–7 present numbers without context—no statistical significance, variance, or training details specific to the detection/segmentation tasks. The learning rate schedule and optimizer settings differ across tasks, but no justification is given. The absence of qualitative results or failure cases further limits interpretability. Such presentation falls short of standards expected at top-tier venues.

- No ablation isolating dual attention components: The paper claims that the dual use of spatial and channel attention at multiple scales is key to performance, but the ablation study (Table 8) does not disable either branch to measure their individual contributions. Readers cannot determine whether gains come from spatial attention, channel attention, or their combination. To support the core claim, the authors should report results for variants that remove the spatial attention module while keeping channel attention (and vice versa), as well as a version without multi-scale feature grouping. This is standard practice in attention mechanism papers and necessary to validate design choices.

- Missing computational complexity analysis of attention maps: The spatial attention module computes an $N \times N$ matrix with $N = H \times W$, which for a $56 \times 56$ feature map results in over 9 million elements per sample—computationally prohibitive for standard hardware. The paper does not address this cost or propose approximations (e.g., low-rank decomposition, local windowing). Without discussing how this is resolved in practice, the method appears infeasible as described. The authors should either revise the formulation or provide empirical runtime and memory usage for the attention blocks to demonstrate practicality.

- No comparison to modern lightweight attention modules: The evaluation omits recent efficient attention designs such as CoordAttention, SimAM, and VoVNet's OSANet, despite claiming superior efficiency and plug-and-play capability. These models are directly comparable in both goal and deployment context. To substantiate novelty and practical advantage, the authors must include side-by-side comparisons on the same backbone (ResNet-50) and dataset (ImageNet), reporting accuracy, FLOPs, parameter count, and ideally inference latency on a common platform such as a GPU or edge device.

- Underspecified feature grouping and shuffling mechanism: The number of groups $G$ and the grouping strategy are not specified, nor is the impact of $G$ on performance or efficiency analyzed. Feature grouping is central to the architecture, yet there is no ablation or discussion of how it affects representational capacity or cross-group communication. The authors should define $G$ explicitly (e.g., 8 or 16 groups), illustrate the shuffling operation, and include an ablation in Table 8 that varies $G$ or removes shuffling to show its effect on accuracy and convergence.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a comprehensive ablation study that isolates the contribution of spatial attention, channel attention, and multi-scale grouping to the final performance.

- Clarify and correct the mathematical formulation of the attention modules, ensuring they are computationally feasible and consistent with the implementation.

- Include comparisons with recent state-of-the-art attention mechanisms such as CoordAttention, SimAM, and SA-Net, and discuss architectural differences.

- Provide a detailed breakdown of computational cost (FLOPs, latency, memory) specifically for the DMSA module, not just the full network.

- Revise the Results section to include discussion, statistical context, and qualitative examples to support the reported metrics.

comments.title: Index collision in channel attention softmax
comments.quote: x_{ji} = \frac{\exp(A_i \cdot A_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^{C} \exp(A_i \cdot A_j)}$ (3)
comments.explanation: The denominator uses $i$ as a summation index, which conflicts with its use as a free index in the numerator. This scoping error makes the expression ambiguous. The correct form should use a distinct dummy index: $x_{ji} = \frac{\exp(A_i \cdot A_j)}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} \exp(A_k \cdot A_j)}$, ensuring normalization over all channels $k$ for fixed $j$.

comments.title: Incorrect indexing in spatial attention softmax
comments.quote: s_{ji} = \frac{\exp(B_i \cdot C_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp(B_i \cdot C_j)}$ (5)
comments.explanation: The index $i$ is used both as a free variable in $B_i$ and as a bound summation index, creating a formal inconsistency. The intended operation—computing attention from position $j$ to all $i$—requires a different indexing structure. The correct form is $s_{ji} = \frac{\exp(B_j \cdot C_i)}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \exp(B_j \cdot C_k)}$, which aligns with row-wise softmax on $B^T C$ and ensures valid normalization.

comments.title: Ill-formed softmax due to index collision in scale weighting
comments.quote: att_i = \mathrm{Softmax}(Z_i) = \frac{\exp(Z_i)}{\sum_{i=0}^{S-1} \exp(Z_i)}$ (8)
comments.explanation: The index $i$ is simultaneously free in $att_i$ and bound in the summation, making the expression mathematically invalid. The softmax must be defined with a separate dummy index: $att_i = \frac{\exp(Z_i)}{\sum_{j=0}^{S-1} \exp(Z_j)}$. This ensures the denominator is a scalar and the output is a proper probability distribution over $S$ scales.

comments.title: Invalid output indexing in channel attention
comments.quote: E_{1j} = \beta \sum_{i=1}^{C} (x_{ji}A_i) + A_j$ (4)
comments.explanation: The subscript $E_{1j}$ incorrectly fixes the spatial dimension to 1, implying only a single spatial location is updated. Since $A_i \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W}$, the sum $\sum_i x_{ji} A_i$ produces a full feature map. The output should be $E_j$, representing the entire $j$-th channel. The current notation suggests a tensor shape error and misrepresents the operation.

comments.title: Mismatch between matrix operation and equation in channel attention
comments.quote: We perform a matrix multiplication between the transpose of $X$ and $A$ and reshape their result to $R^{C \times H \times W}$.
comments.explanation: The text describes $X^T A$, but Equation 4 uses $\sum_i x_{ji} A_i$, which corresponds to $X A$, not $X^T A$. These operations are not equivalent unless $X$ is symmetric, which is not guaranteed. The matrix multiplication must be corrected to $X A$ to match the summation, or the equation must be adjusted to reflect $X^T A$. The current mismatch breaks the mathematical consistency of the module.

comments.title: Unjustified subscript in spatial attention output
comments.quote: E_{2j} = \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{N} (s_{ji}D_i) + A_j$ (6)
comments.explanation: The notation $E_{2j}$ introduces an unexplained '2' subscript, suggesting a branch index, but this module is presented in isolation. If '2' refers to the spatial branch in a dual-attention setup, that context is missing here. Within the spatial attention block, the output should be simply $E_j$. Retaining $E_{2j}$ creates ambiguity and breaks notational consistency with the channel attention formulation.

comments.title: Mismatch between softmax and feature application in multi-scale fusion
comments.quote: Y_i = F_i \odot att_i \quad i=1,2,3,\cdots S-1$ (9)
comments.explanation: Equation 8 defines $att_i$ for $i=0$ to $S-1$, but Equation 9 starts at $i=1$, leaving $att_0$ unused and $F_0$ undefined. This indexing gap breaks the alignment between attention weights and features. The range should be consistent: $i=0,1,\dots,S-1$. Additionally, $F_i$ is never defined—Equation 7 produces a single $F$, not a sequence. The multi-scale inputs must be explicitly introduced before this step.

comments.title: Misattribution of dual attention architecture
comments.quote: These two branches are comprised of position attention module and channel attention module as proposed in (Fu et al., 2019) for semantic segmentation.
comments.explanation: Fu et al. (2019) proposed Dual Attention Network (DANet), which uses position (spatial) attention and a separate context aggregation module, but does not include a parallel channel attention branch as described here. Attributing a dual-branch spatial-channel design to that work is incorrect. The proposed architecture may draw inspiration from DANet, but the specific parallel structure is not from the cited source. This misattribution undermines the paper's scholarly accuracy.

comments.title: Inconsistent learning rate specification
comments.quote: Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of  $1e^{-4}$ , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.
comments.explanation: The text states the learning rate is both $1e^{-4}$ and 0.1, which is contradictory. Given that 0.1 is standard for ImageNet training with ResNet-50, and $1e^{-4}$ is typical for weight decay, the former is likely the intended initial learning rate. The $1e^{-4}$ value probably refers to weight decay, which is specified in later sections but omitted here. Clarify the hyperparameters to avoid confusion.

comments.title: Missing weight decay for classification task
comments.quote: Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of  $1e^{-4}$ , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64.
comments.explanation: Weight decay is a critical hyperparameter for stable training of ResNet-50 on ImageNet, yet it is not specified in the classification setup. It is mentioned for detection and segmentation tasks ('weight decay of $1e^{-4}$') but omitted here. To ensure reproducibility and consistency, explicitly state the weight decay value used in the classification experiments.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter reporting across tables
comments.explanation: The reported parameter count for ResNet-50 (41.53M) in Table 4 differs from the value in Table 2 (25.56M). This discrepancy suggests either inconsistent model definitions or inclusion of detector head parameters in Table 4 without clarification. If the higher count includes the detection head, this should be explicitly stated. Otherwise, the inconsistency undermines confidence in the reported metrics.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of lower computational cost
comments.quote: Our network shows the best performance in accuracy, achieving a considerable improvement compared with all the previous attention models along with lower computational cost.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'lower computational cost' is not supported by evidence in this section. While Tables 2 and 3 are referenced, the reduction in FLOPs (e.g., 3.44G vs 4.12G) likely reflects changes in the backbone or grouping strategy, not the attention module alone. Without isolating the cost of the DMSA block, this claim is misleading. The paper should provide a breakdown of the attention module's FLOPs and parameters to substantiate efficiency claims.

comments.title: Ablation study misrepresents its own findings
comments.quote: The ablation experiments show that our attention module captures long-range contextual information effectively at various scales thus making it generalizable to other tasks.
comments.explanation: Table 8 only evaluates normalization variants and $F_c()$ replacements—it does not ablate the core components of DMSANet such as the dual attention branches or multi-scale grouping. Therefore, it cannot support the conclusion that the attention module itself captures long-range context or enables generalization. The actual ablation is too limited to justify such broad claims. The text should accurately reflect the scope of the study.

comments.title: Overstated performance claim without cross-task evidence
comments.quote: After evaluating our network both qualitatively and quantitatively, we show that our network outperforms previous state of the art across image classification, object
comments.explanation: The only quantitative results provided in the ablation section (Table 8) are for image classification. No ablation or analysis is presented for object detection or instance segmentation. Therefore, the claim of outperforming prior work across all three tasks lacks evidentiary support in this section. The statement should be limited to the tasks for which results are actually shown.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology: 'position' vs 'spatial' attention
comments.quote: second made up of position and channel attention modules in parallel for capturing global contextual information.
comments.explanation: The term 'position attention' is used in the introduction and conclusion, but all technical sections refer to 'spatial attention'. This inconsistency creates confusion about whether these are distinct mechanisms. Given that 'spatial attention' is the standard term and used throughout the method description, 'position attention' should be replaced to maintain terminological coherence.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes DMSANet, a dual multi-scale attention network that combines parallel spatial and channel attention with multi-scale feature aggregation. While the reported results show impressive improvements across ImageNet classification, COCO detection, and segmentation tasks, several critical methodological and evaluative gaps undermine the paper's contributions.

The paper presents a potentially interesting attention mechanism that claims to achieve state-of-the-art performance with reduced computational cost. The architecture combines existing ideas (feature grouping, parallel attention, multi-scale processing) in a novel way. However, the evaluation is incomplete, the ablation studies are insufficient, and the claimed efficiency advantages lack proper verification.

Overview issues:

- Missing ablation study for multi-scale component: The paper claims multi-scale feature aggregation as a key contribution but provides no ablation study to validate its importance. Table 8 only tests normalization choices and the Fc() operation, but never compares the full model against a version without multi-scale processing. This makes it impossible to determine whether the performance gains come from the multi-scale design or just the parallel attention mechanism. The authors should include results showing the impact of removing the multi-scale feature extraction and aggregation components.

- Unsubstantiated computational efficiency claims: The paper claims DMSANet has 'lesser parameters' and 'lesser computational cost' but the FLOPs in Tables 2-6 show a different story. For ResNet-50, DMSANet uses 26.25M parameters vs 25.56M for baseline ResNet - a 2.7% increase. More concerning, the FLOPs for object detection (Table 4) show DMSANet at 222.31 GFLOPs vs 207.07 for ResNet-50 baseline - a 7.4% increase. The paper needs to reconcile these numbers with its efficiency claims or adjust the claims to reflect the actual computational overhead.

- Incomplete comparison with recent attention mechanisms: The paper lacks comparisons with several important recent attention mechanisms. Notably absent are comparisons with Swin Transformer and ConvNeXt architectures, which represent the current state-of-the-art. The comparisons in Figure 1 and Tables 2-3 stop at EPSANet (2021), missing more recent developments. For a paper claiming 'best performance' to be credible in 2022, it must compare against the most recent and competitive baselines, particularly transformer-based approaches that have dominated recent benchmarks.

- Insufficient methodological details for reproducibility: The paper lacks critical implementation details needed for reproducibility. Section 3.6 mentions hyperparameters but doesn't specify: the number of groups G for feature grouping, the reduction ratio C_r in channel attention, or the kernel sizes used in the multi-scale feature extraction. The architecture diagram in Figure 2 is too high-level to understand the exact operations. Without these details, reproducing the reported 80.02% Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet would be extremely difficult.

- No analysis of attention mechanism effectiveness: The paper provides no visualization or analysis of the learned attention maps. For an attention mechanism paper, showing qualitative evidence that the module is focusing on relevant features is standard practice. The absence of attention map visualizations makes it impossible to verify whether the dual multi-scale design is actually capturing the intended local and global dependencies. The authors should include attention visualizations on sample images to demonstrate the mechanism's behavior.

- Missing demonstration of multi-scale effectiveness: The paper claims that aggregating features at various scales is a key contribution, but provides no concrete demonstration that this multi-scale processing actually captures meaningful information. A worked example showing how different scales contribute to the final attention weights would strengthen the paper. Specifically, the authors should compute the multi-scale attention weights (Equation 8) for a sample input and show how different scales are selected for different image regions. This would help readers understand whether the multi-scale design is truly learning scale-adaptive behavior rather than just aggregating features uniformly.

- No computational complexity analysis of dual attention: The paper claims the dual attention design is lightweight but provides no analysis of the computational overhead of the parallel position and channel attention modules. The matrix multiplications in Equations 3-6 for computing attention maps have O(N²) complexity where N=H×W, which could be prohibitive for high-resolution inputs. The authors should analyze the computational complexity of their attention mechanism and compare it with single-branch alternatives. Without this analysis, readers cannot assess whether the dual design is truly efficient or just appears lightweight at low resolutions.

- Missing comparison with baseline architectures: The paper never shows that their dual attention design actually recovers existing attention mechanisms as special cases, despite borrowing components from prior work. For example, setting the number of groups G=1 should reduce to a standard attention module. The authors should demonstrate that their formulation generalizes existing approaches by showing how SE-Net or CBAM can be obtained from their architecture with specific parameter settings. This would validate their claim of providing a more general framework rather than just another variant.

- No guidance on hyperparameter selection: The paper provides no guidance on selecting critical hyperparameters like the number of groups G or the reduction ratio C_r in channel attention. These choices significantly affect both performance and computational cost. The authors should include a sensitivity analysis showing how performance varies with different values of G and C_r, along with recommendations for practitioners. Without this, readers cannot determine appropriate settings for their own applications, limiting the module's claimed ease of integration.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper reports strong quantitative results but has significant methodological gaps. The missing ablation for the multi-scale component, questionable efficiency claims, and lack of recent baselines prevent acceptance in current form. However, the core idea has merit and could be publishable with substantial revisions addressing these issues.

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive ablation studies isolating the contribution of each component (multi-scale aggregation, parallel attention, feature grouping)

- Reconcile efficiency claims with reported FLOPs/parameters or adjust claims accordingly

- Include comparisons with recent state-of-the-art methods like Swin Transformer and ConvNeXt

- Provide attention map visualizations and analysis to demonstrate mechanism effectiveness

- Add missing implementation details (group count, reduction ratios, kernel sizes) for reproducibility

comments.title: Contradictory learning rate specifications
comments.quote: Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of  $1e^{-4}$ , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.
comments.explanation: The text contains a direct contradiction about the initial learning rate. The first sentence states the learning rate is 1e^{-4}, but the second sentence states it's initially set as 0.1. Since 0.1 is the standard initial learning rate for ImageNet training with ResNet, the 1e^{-4} value is likely a typo. Remove the conflicting 'learning rate of 1e^{-4}' phrase to resolve this inconsistency.

comments.title: Index inconsistency in attention equation
comments.quote: $att_{i}=\mathrm{Softmax}\left(Z_{i}\right)=\frac{\exp\left(Z_{i}\right)}{\sum_{i=0}^{S-1}\exp\left(Z_{i}\right)}$ (8)
comments.explanation: The equation uses index i both as a free variable on the left side and as the summation index on the right side, creating a mathematical inconsistency. Rewrite as $att_{i}=\mathrm{Softmax}\left(Z_{i}\right)=\frac{\exp\left(Z_{i}\right)}{\sum_{j=0}^{S-1}\exp\left(Z_{j}\right)}$ because the current notation makes it unclear whether att_i represents the i-th component of the attention vector or the entire attention vector.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of prior work
comments.quote: There are two main problems which hinders the progress in this field: 1) Both spatial and channel attention as well as network using combination of two uses only local information while ignoring long range channel dependency, 2) The previous architectures fail to capture spatial information at different scales to be more robust and handle more complex problems.
comments.explanation: This characterization is inaccurate. Several prior attention mechanisms explicitly address these issues. Non-local Neural Networks (Wang et al., 2018) was specifically designed to capture long-range dependencies, not just local information. Similarly, Selective Kernel Networks (Li et al., 2019) and Pyramid Split Attention (Zhang et al., 2021) directly tackle multi-scale feature processing. The claim that previous architectures 'ignore long range channel dependency' and 'fail to capture spatial information at different scales' misrepresents the actual contributions of these seminal works.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated efficiency claims
comments.quote: Our proposed network achieves higher accuracy while having less model complexity.
comments.explanation: This claim in Figure 1's caption contradicts the actual data presented in the paper's tables. According to Table 2, DMSANet with ResNet-50 uses 26.25M parameters compared to 25.56M for baseline ResNet-50, representing an increase in model complexity. Similarly, Table 4 shows DMSANet at 222.31 GFLOPs vs 207.07 for ResNet-50 baseline. The authors should either correct the figure caption to reflect the actual parameter increase or remove this misleading claim about having 'less model complexity'.

comments.title: Index range mismatch in equations
comments.quote: $Y_{i}=F_{i}\odot att_{i}\quad i=1,2,3,\cdots S-1$ (9)
comments.explanation: Equation 9 specifies the index range as i=1,2,3,...,S-1, but Equation 8 uses i=0,2,...,S-1 in its summation. This creates an inconsistency in the indexing scheme. If Equation 8's summation starts from i=0, then Equation 9 should either include i=0 or Equation 8 should start from i=1. Rewrite $Y_{i}=F_{i}\odot att_{i}\quad i=0,1,2,\cdots S-1$ because the current mismatch suggests either F_0 or att_0 would be undefined or unused.

comments.title: Undefined variables in equations
comments.quote:
$att_{i}=\mathrm{Softmax}\left(Z_{i}\right)=\frac{\exp\left(Z_{i}\right)}{\sum_{i=0}^{S-1}\exp\left(Z_{i}\right)}$ (8)

Finally we multiply the re-calibrated weight of multi-scale channel attention $att_{i}$ with the feature map of the corresponding scale $F_{i}$ as shown in Equation 9:

$Y_{i}=F_{i}\odot att_{i}\quad i=1,2,3,\cdots S-1$ (9)
comments.explanation: The equations reference variables Z_i and F_i that are not defined in this section. Equation 7 defines F as a concatenated feature map, but Equation 9 uses F_i as individual scale feature maps. Similarly, Z_i appears in Equation 8 without definition. Add 'where Z_i represents the feature descriptor values for scale i and F_i represents the individual feature maps before concatenation (E_{1j}, E_{2j})' after Equation 8 because the text should clarify these definitions.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in ablation explanation
comments.quote: Both removing $F_{c}()$ and using $1 \times 1$ Conv results in reduced performance as compared to the original network. The earlier is because $F_{c}()$ is used to enhance the performance of individual features while latter is because number of channels in each sub-feature is too few, so it is not important to exchange information among different channels.
comments.explanation: The explanation contains a logical contradiction. The paper states that using 1×1 Conv reduces performance because 'number of channels in each sub-feature is too few, so it is not important to exchange information among different channels.' However, if channel exchange is unimportant due to few channels, then removing the 1×1 Conv (which performs channel exchange) should not hurt performance. The reasoning contradicts the observed result that removing 1×1 Conv reduces performance. Rewrite the explanation to clarify why 1×1 Conv is beneficial despite having few channels.

comments.title: Inconsistent group notation in feature splitting
comments.quote: For a given feature map $X\in R^{C\times H\times W}$ , where $C,H,W$ indicate the channel number, spatial height, and width, respectively, shuffle attention module divides $X$ into $G$ groups along the channel dimension, i.e., $X=[X_{1},X_{G}],X_{k}\in R^{C/G\times H\times W}$. An attention module is used to weight the importance of each feature. The input of $X_{k}$ is split into two networks along the channel dimension $X_{k1},X_{k2}\in R^{C/2G\times H\times W}$.
comments.explanation: The notation $X=[X_{1},X_{G}]$ is mathematically inconsistent. When dividing into G groups, the correct notation should be $X=[X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{G}]$ to show all G groups. The current notation only shows the first and last group, which could be misinterpreted as having only 2 groups. Rewrite the passage as: 'i.e., $X=[X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{G}], X_{k}\in R^{C/G\times H\times W}$' and '$X_{k1},X_{k2}\in R^{C/(2G)\times H\times W}$' to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Missing weight decay specification for ImageNet
comments.quote: Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of  $1e^{-4}$ , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.
comments.explanation: The ImageNet training configuration omits the weight decay parameter, which is specified for object detection (1e^{-4}) and instance segmentation (1e^{-4}) but not for image classification. Weight decay is a critical hyperparameter that significantly affects training dynamics and final performance. The authors should add the weight decay value used for ImageNet training to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Channel shuffle operation undefined
comments.quote: We use a ”channel shuffle” operator to enable cross-group information flow along the channel dimension.
comments.explanation: The text mentions using a channel shuffle operator but provides no mathematical definition or reference to how it's implemented. Add a formal definition such as 'Channel shuffle rearranges channels according to a permutation matrix P: F' = Reshape(Permute(Reshape(F, (G, C/G, H, W))), (C, H, W)) where G is the number of groups' after the first sentence because readers need to understand this operation to reproduce the method.

comments.title: Inconsistent channel dimension notation
comments.quote: An input feature map $X$ is splitted into $N$ parts along with the channel dimension. For each splitted parts, it has $C_{0}=C_{S}$ number of common channels, and the $i^{th}$ feature map is $X_{i}\in R^{C_{0}\times H\times W}$.
comments.explanation: The notation $C_{0}=C_{S}$ is ambiguous. The text states that $X$ is split into $N$ parts along the channel dimension, and each part has $C_{0}$ channels. This implies the total channels $C = N \times C_{0}$. However, the equation $C_{0}=C_{S}$ is introduced without defining $C_{S}$. Rewrite the sentence as: 'For each split part, it has $C_{0}=C/N$ number of channels, where $C$ is the total number of channels in the input feature map $X$, and the $i^{th}$ feature map is $X_{i}\in R^{C_{0}\times H\times W}$.' because this clarifies the relationship between total channels and split channels.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
DMSANet proposes a dual multi-scale attention network combining spatial and channel attention in parallel branches to improve feature representation. The authors claim superior performance on ImageNet, COCO detection, and segmentation tasks with reduced parameter counts compared to existing attention-based models.

The paper provides a clear architectural proposal and demonstrates competitive empirical results across several standard vision benchmarks. However, the evaluation lacks sufficient depth regarding computational efficiency in real-world deployment, and the justification for the specific multi-scale aggregation strategy remains largely heuristic.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Real-World Efficiency Analysis: The authors emphasize that DMSANet is 'lightweight' and 'easily integrated,' yet they only provide FLOPs and parameter counts as proxies for efficiency. In modern deep learning, these metrics often fail to capture real-world latency, especially for custom attention modules that may not be well-optimized for standard hardware kernels. To support the claim of efficiency, the paper should include inference speed measurements (e.g., ms per image) on standard hardware (e.g., NVIDIA T4 or A100) compared to the baseline ResNet and other attention-based models.

- Insufficient Justification for Multi-Scale Aggregation: Section 3.4 describes the aggregation of sub-features using a 'channel shuffle' operator and a weighted sum, but provides little theoretical or empirical justification for this specific design choice. It is unclear why this aggregation order is optimal compared to lateral connections or cross-stage fusion methods used in FPN or recent multi-scale attention literature. The authors should include an ablation study comparing their aggregation method against simpler alternatives, such as direct concatenation or standard residual connections, to prove that the proposed weighted aggregation is the source of the performance gain.

- Overfitting and Generalization Concerns: The performance gains reported in Table 2 and Table 3 are significant, raising questions about whether these attention modules are over-tuned to the ImageNet dataset. The paper lacks a discussion on how the attention weights behave across different input resolutions or domains, such as small object detection in aerial imagery where scale variation is extreme. Including a qualitative visualization of attention maps across different object sizes would help clarify whether the network is truly learning scale-invariant features or simply overfitting to the specific distribution of the COCO dataset.

- Ambiguity in Attention Module Redundancy: The proposed architecture employs both spatial and channel attention in parallel. Given that these modules often capture overlapping information, there is a risk of parameter redundancy that could be pruned without performance loss. The paper does not analyze the information overlap between these two branches, nor does it explore the sensitivity of the model to the number of groups G used in the shuffle attention module. A sensitivity analysis of the hyperparameter G would provide much-needed evidence that the architecture is robust and not just a result of hyperparameter tuning.

- Absence of empirical latency and throughput benchmarks: The paper repeatedly claims the DMSANet module is 'lightweight' and efficient, yet it relies exclusively on theoretical FLOPs and parameter counts to support this. These metrics often fail to reflect actual inference speed on modern hardware due to memory access patterns and kernel overheads specific to attention mechanisms. To validate the claim of efficiency, the authors should report actual wall-clock inference latency (in milliseconds) and throughput (images per second) for a fixed batch size on a standard GPU, such as an NVIDIA V100 or A100, comparing DMSANet against the baseline ResNet and the SOTA EPSANet.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for the grouping hyperparameter G: The architecture relies on a grouping strategy where the input feature map is divided into G groups, yet the paper treats G as a fixed constant without justifying its choice. It is unclear if the performance gains are robust to variations in G or if they are sensitive to the specific scale of the feature maps at different network depths. The paper should include a sensitivity analysis, reporting Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet for a range of G values (e.g., G=2, 4, 8, 16) to demonstrate that the proposed design is not merely the result of over-tuning this hyperparameter.

- Need for qualitative evidence of multi-scale feature capture: The authors claim that the module effectively captures long-range contextual information at various scales, but they provide no visual evidence to support this mechanism. Readers cannot verify if the attention weights are actually focusing on diverse spatial scales or if the module is simply learning a global bias. The paper would be significantly more complete if it included representative heatmaps or Grad-CAM visualizations showing how the attention mechanism responds to objects of varying sizes in the COCO dataset, specifically highlighting the differences between the spatial and channel attention branches.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include an empirical latency analysis (ms per image) on standard GPU hardware to substantiate the 'lightweight' efficiency claims.

- Perform an ablation study comparing the proposed weighted aggregation method against standard concatenation and residual fusion to isolate the module's contribution.

- Provide qualitative visualizations of attention maps for objects of varying scales to demonstrate that the multi-scale mechanism is functioning as intended.

- Conduct a sensitivity analysis on the grouping hyperparameter G to demonstrate the robustness of the architecture.

comments.title: Contradictory claim regarding parameter reduction
comments.quote: The channel attention module is used to selectively weight the importance of each channel and thus produces best output features. This helps in reducing the number of parameters of the network.
comments.explanation: The channel attention module as defined uses fully-connected layers and matrix multiplications, which inherently increase the parameter count. The claim that this module reduces the network's parameters is unsupported and contradicts the structural definition provided. Please revise this to acknowledge that the module introduces additional parameters to enable adaptive recalibration.
